content.text: is a programming model where workflow state is automatically persisted after every step. If the process crashes or is redeployed, execution resumes exactly where it left off — completed steps are not re-run, and their side effects (LLM calls, database writes, HTTP requests) are not repeated.

attrs.alt: Durable execution: Worker 1 crashes at step 3, Worker 2 replays steps 1+2 from journal then continues from step 3

content.text: Why Agentic AI Needs Durable Execution

A multi-step AI agent that calls an LLM ten times, queries a database, writes results, and sends emails may take minutes to hours. Standard async code fails silently on any process crash, OOM kill, or deploy — wasting API tokens, corrupting partial writes, and forcing the user to start over.

# Problem: long-running LLM agent without durable execution

import anthropic

def run_research_agent(topic: str) -> str:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    # Step 1 — 30-second LLM call
    outline = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=1000,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Create a 10-section outline for: {topic}"}],
    ).content[0].text

    # Step 2 — iterate over 10 sections (each section = another 30s LLM call)
    sections = []
    for i, section in enumerate(outline.split("\n")[:10]):
        content = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-8",
            max_tokens=500,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Write section {i+1}: {section}"}],
        ).content[0].text
        sections.append(content)
        # If the process crashes HERE (OOM, timeout, deploy), all prior work is lost.
        # On retry, ALL 10 LLM calls run again — wasting tokens and time.

    return "\n\n".join(sections)
OOM kills, hardware failures, VM preemption on spot instances — common in production. An agent with 10 sequential LLM calls fails before finishing 30% of the time on spot compute.
rolling deploys kill running processes. A 20-minute research agent started at deploy time will be killed halfway through.
LLM APIs rate-limit at random steps. Retrying from the start wastes all successfully completed tokens and time.
any step with a side effect (LLM call, HTTP request, DB write, email send) is registered as an
. The engine executes each activity exactly once and persists the result to a journal before moving on.
the orchestration logic (loops, conditionals, fan-out) is a
— it may not call external services directly. It only calls activities.
on resume, the engine replays completed activities from the journal (returning their cached results instantly) until it reaches the first not-yet-completed step, then continues executing live.
workflows can sleep for hours or days (
) without holding a process open. The engine wakes the workflow when the timer fires.
Temporal is the most widely adopted open-source durable execution engine. It runs a Temporal Server (the journal + scheduler) and one or more Workers (your Python code).
Deterministic orchestration function — no I/O, no randomness, no time.time()
Side-effectful function — LLM calls, HTTP requests, DB writes go here
Named channel; workers poll for tasks; workflows and activities share a queue name
User-supplied unique identifier; used to deduplicate and query running workflows
Asynchronous message sent to a running workflow to change its state
Synchronous read of a workflow's state without affecting it
Durable sleep — the worker process does not need to stay alive
Per-activity exponential backoff with jitter, max attempts, non-retryable errors

from datetime import timedelta
# Required imports for the above example
# pip install temporalio anthropic

, no direct network calls. All non-determinism lives inside activities. The Temporal SDK enforces this at runtime by replaying the workflow code — if it produces different decisions than the journal recorded, Temporal raises a non-determinism error.

import asyncio
from temporalio.client import Client
from temporalio.worker import Worker

async def main():
    client = await Client.connect("localhost:7233")

    # Start a workflow — returns immediately with a handle
    handle = await client.start_workflow(
        ResearchWorkflow.run,
        "Quantum Computing for Software Engineers",
        id="research-qc-001",
        task_queue="research-queue",
    )
    print(f"Workflow started: {handle.id}")

    # Wait for the result (can be hours later — Temporal persists state)
    result = await handle.result()
    print(result[:200])

asyncio.run(main())
Temporal persists the workflow handle in the server's database. The
call can be made from a different process hours later and will block until the workflow completes — even across server restarts.
Signals allow external callers to change a running workflow's behavior (pause, inject data, cancel). Queries allow reading current state without modifying it.
human-in-the-loop approval steps (pause until a human reviews), injecting dynamic configuration mid-run, cancellation with cleanup.
status dashboards, progress meters, debugging live workflows without stopping them.

from temporalio.common import RetryPolicy
from datetime import timedelta

# Configure per-activity retry behaviour
retry = RetryPolicy(
    initial_interval=timedelta(seconds=1),
    backoff_coefficient=2.0,
    maximum_interval=timedelta(minutes=2),
    maximum_attempts=10,
    # Do not retry on 4xx client errors — these are bugs, not transient failures
    non_retryable_error_types=["anthropic.BadRequestError", "ValueError"],
)

# Apply in workflow:
result = await workflow.execute_activity(
    my_llm_activity,
    args=["prompt text"],
    schedule_to_close_timeout=timedelta(minutes=10),
    retry_policy=retry,
)
the outer deadline for the entire activity (all retries included). Set this to the worst-case time you'll wait, not the per-attempt timeout.
per-attempt timeout. If the activity runs for longer than this, Temporal cancels and retries it.
always list 4xx client errors (invalid prompt, context too long) as non-retryable. Retrying them wastes tokens and delays failure reporting.
Temporal requires running a server cluster. Lighter-weight alternatives exist for teams that need durable guarantees without the operational overhead.

# Durable execution alternatives to Temporal

# 1. Restate (Python SDK)
#    pip install restate-sdk
#    Restate uses an HTTP-based journal — activities are journaled as HTTP responses.
#    Suitable for: serverless environments, smaller teams without Kubernetes.

# 2. Inngest (TypeScript / Python SDK)
#    pip install inngest
#    Hosted durable execution — no cluster to run. Pay-per-step pricing.
#    Suitable for: SaaS products that cannot self-host a Temporal cluster.

# 3. Prefect / Dagster
#    Task-level checkpointing (not full durable execution, but close enough
#    for batch data pipelines). Activities are retried from the failed task,
#    not replayed from a journal.
#    Suitable for: ML pipelines and ETL flows, not long-running conversational agents.

# 4. AWS Step Functions
#    Managed state machine service. Each state transition is durable.
#    Supports long-running workflows (up to 1 year).
#    Suitable for: AWS-native stacks, when you want managed infrastructure.

calling an LLM or making an HTTP request directly in the workflow function (not wrapped in an activity) breaks replay. On the second run, the result may differ — Temporal raises a non-determinism error.
directly in workflow code produces different values on replay. Use
for time and pass seeds in via the workflow input.

an activity that does 10 LLM calls internally is still atomic from Temporal's perspective — if it fails at call 8, all 10 re-run on retry. Break long activities into one-call-per-activity to get fine-grained checkpointing.
Temporal requires at least one timeout on every activity. Without it, a hanging HTTP call blocks a worker slot indefinitely.
Temporal server as single point of failure:
run Temporal Server with a replicated database (CockroachDB or Postgres with replication) and at least two frontend pods in production. A single-node development setup is not production-safe.
persists workflow state after every activity, enabling automatic resume after crashes, deploys, and rate-limit retries.
(side-effectful steps: LLM calls, DB writes, HTTP).
Completed activities are replayed from the journal on resume — their side effects do not repeat, and no LLM tokens are re-billed.
let external callers change workflow state mid-run;
read state without affecting it — enabling human-in-the-loop patterns and progress dashboards.
For teams that cannot self-host Temporal,
(managed SaaS) provide the same guarantee with lower operational overhead.
